metadata.purpose: Canonical plant caution/look-alike entries used by related species links and caution views.

records.danger_level: Unsafe look-alike for ostrich fern fiddleheads
records.poisoning_effects: Do not substitute bracken fern for ostrich fern fiddleheads. Bracken has documented toxicity concerns and has been shown to cause cancer in animal studies; it is also a known livestock problem.
records.field_identification: Bracken fiddleheads are fuzzy, lack the brown papery covering of ostrich fern fiddleheads, and do not have the deep U-shaped groove on the inside of the stem.
records.edibility_detail: Not treated as a food species in this guide. Included here as a look-alike warning for Fiddlehead Ferns.
records.look_alike_notes: Can be confused by beginners with edible ostrich fern fiddleheads. Do not harvest fern fiddleheads unless the ostrich fern traits are confirmed.

use_links.label: University of Maine Extension: Facts on Fiddleheads

records.field_identification: A common ostrich fern look-alike. Do not substitute it for edible ostrich fern fiddleheads; confirm the smooth stem, brown papery scales, and deep U-shaped groove of ostrich fern.
records.edibility_detail: Not treated as an edible species in this guide. Included here as a look-alike warning for Fiddlehead Ferns.
records.look_alike_notes: Commonly confused with ostrich fern by beginners; not recommended as a substitute food fiddlehead.

records.danger_level: Deadly Apiaceae look-alike for edible/caution carrot-family plants
records.poisoning_effects: Highly poisonous; can cause serious nervous-system poisoning and death if eaten.
records.field_identification: Tall smooth-stemmed carrot-family plant with purple or reddish-brown spotting/blotching on hollow stems, ferny divided leaves, white umbels, and a strong unpleasant/mousey odor. Lacks the bristly hairy stem of wild carrot.
records.edibility_detail: DEADLY POISON — do not eat, taste, chew, or use for tea. Poison hemlock can cause severe nervous-system poisoning, respiratory failure, and death. Included as a searchable deadly Apiaceae warning record, not as a food plant.
records.look_alike_notes: Deadly Apiaceae warning. Smooth hairless hollow stems with purple/red blotches, ferny divided leaves, and a mousey odor point toward poison hemlock. Do not use white umbels or carrot-family leaf shape as edible ID.

records.hard_rule: Tall white-umbel Apiaceae with smooth, hollow, hairless stems and purple/red blotches is a hard stop. Do not confuse poison hemlock with wild carrot, angelica, sweet cicely, or water hemlock.

field_measurements.overall: Mature stems 2–10 ft tall / about 0.6–3 m
field_measurements.leaf: Rosette leaves can be over 2 ft long; stem leaves 1–16 in long
field_measurements.stem: Hollow, hairless stems with purple or reddish-brown blotches
field_measurements.notes: Size source: Cornell Weed ID. Toxicity and Apiaceae hard-stop warning remain separate.

records.poisoning_effects: Deadly poisonous; ingestion can cause severe nervous-system poisoning, seizures, respiratory failure, and death.
records.field_identification: Wetland carrot-family plant with white umbels, divided leaves, and extremely poisonous roots/stem bases. Often found in wet soils, ditches, stream banks, pond margins, and marshes. Do not handle, taste, or smell-test roots for identification.
records.edibility_detail: DEADLY POISON — do not eat, taste, chew, smell-test, or dig unknown wetland Apiaceae roots. Water hemlock roots and stem bases are especially dangerous, and poisoning can progress rapidly to vomiting, convulsions/seizures, respiratory failure, and death.
records.look_alike_notes: Deadly wetland Apiaceae warning. White umbels and divided leaves can overlap with edible/caution carrot-family plants; wet habitat and root/stem-base uncertainty are hard stop traits.

records.scope_note: Broad Cicuta spp. warning record; Cicuta maculata is the representative eastern/Great Lakes water-hemlock source anchor, with Cicuta bulbifera included as another regional Cicuta source reference.
records.hard_rule: Wetland, ditch, shoreline, streambank, or marsh Apiaceae with white umbels is a hard stop unless expertly identified. Unknown wetland carrot-family roots stay out of the mouth.

field_measurements.overall: Tall stems up to 6 ft / about 1.8 m
field_measurements.root: Root crown has internal chambers/partitions; do not handle, taste, or cut for ID in the field
field_measurements.notes: Size/source context: US Forest Service. Deadly toxicity warning remains separate from size.

records.non_edible_severity: Dangerous sap — severe sunlight-triggered burn risk
records.danger_level: Dangerous Apiaceae contact-burn plant and Wild carrot look-alike
records.poisoning_effects: Sap from leaves, stems, flowers, and fruit can cause phytophotodermatitis: severe sunlight-triggered burning, blistering, rash, and long-lasting discoloration. This is a chemical/phototoxic burn reaction, not poison-ivy-style allergy. Ingestion risk is mainly from dangerous Apiaceae misidentification; do not taste unknown roots.
affected_systems: eyes/face if sap contacts exposed areas
records.field_identification: Non-native biennial Apiaceae with first-year basal leaves and a second-year flowering stalk. Look for yellow to yellow-green flat-topped compound umbels, 5-15 coarse toothed leaflets, a stout hollow grooved stem, and sunny disturbed habitat such as roadsides, fields, trailsides, and open meadows.
records.notes: Same species as cultivated parsnip, but wild/escaped plants are treated as a serious caution record because above-ground sap can burn skin after sun exposure and Apiaceae root mistakes can be deadly.
records.edibility_detail: Dangerous contact-burn plant. Do not handle wild parsnip bare-handed. Sap from leaves, stems, flowers, and fruit can cause phytophotodermatitis — severe rash, burns, blisters, and long-lasting discoloration after sunlight exposure. The only food-side use is a positively identified first-year taproot with gloves and skin protection.
records.culinary_uses: Edible root only, mainly first-year taproot, after certain identification. Do not handle bare-handed; avoid sap contact; do not taste unknown Apiaceae roots.
records.medicinal_uses: Not treated as a medicinal plant in this guide.
records.plant_card_note: Dangerous contact-burn Apiaceae. First-year taproot is edible only with certain ID and skin protection; flowering/second-year plants are mainly a burn/contact warning, not a beginner food.

plant_part_details.season_or_stage: fall after first-year growth or early spring before flowering stalk develops
plant_part_details.use: edible root with expert-level identification only
plant_part_details.warning: Do not dig or taste unknown Apiaceae roots. Gloves and skin protection are still needed because damaged foliage/stems can release phototoxic sap.

plant_part_details.season_or_stage: growing season, especially flowering and seed stages
plant_part_details.use: not treated as food in this guide
plant_part_details.warning: Sap can cause severe phytophotodermatitis when skin is exposed to sunlight. Wear gloves, long sleeves, and eye/face protection when handling.

use_links.label: Utah State Extension: Wild parsnip identification and control

records.look_alike_notes: Wild parsnip can be confused with other carrot-family plants. Yellow to yellow-green umbels help separate it from white-flowered wild carrot, poison hemlock, water hemlock, angelica, and cow parsnip, but sap-burn risk makes gloves and skin protection mandatory.
records.risk_reason: Wild parsnip is edible-root adjacent but dangerous because the above-ground sap causes severe sun-triggered burns, and Apiaceae root misidentification can be deadly.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Yellow/greenish compound umbels, grooved hollow stems, compound leaves, roadside/open habitats, and edible-root history can mislead people into treating it casually.
records.hard_rule: Do not handle wild parsnip bare-handed. Sap exposure plus sunlight can burn skin; unknown Apiaceae roots stay out of the mouth.

lookalike_risk.label: High — sap burns plus dangerous Apiaceae confusion
lookalike_risk.summary: Wild parsnip has an edible taproot, but the plant is not casual food: above-ground sap can cause severe sun-triggered burns, and Apiaceae root mistakes can be deadly.
required_checks: Confirm yellow wild-parsnip umbels and coarse compound leaves; do not rely on the name parsnip alone.
Wear gloves, long sleeves, and eye/face protection when handling or digging.
Wash sap-exposed skin immediately and keep it out of sunlight.
Rule out poison hemlock and water hemlock before considering any root use.
Avoid flowering/second-year plants for food; the food-side use is first-year taproot only.
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Sap exposure plus sunlight can burn skin; unknown Apiaceae roots stay out of the mouth.

records.scientific_name: Taxus species, especially Taxus canadensis and ornamental yews
records.danger_level: Poisonous evergreen look-alike for tea-use conifers
records.poisoning_effects: Poisonous; most parts of yew contain toxic compounds. Do not use as tea or food.
records.field_identification: Flat evergreen needles on a shrub or small tree; red arils may be present. Do not confuse yew with pine, spruce, fir, or eastern hemlock tea records.
records.edibility_detail: Not treated as an edible species in this guide. Included as a searchable caution/look-alike warning record.
records.look_alike_notes: Use this record to separate dangerous or not-recommended look-alikes from similar edible/tea-use records.

records.danger_level: Poisonous wetland look-alike for staghorn sumac
records.poisoning_effects: Causes severe allergic/contact dermatitis from urushiol exposure; do not harvest or handle as edible sumac.
records.field_identification: Wetland shrub/small tree with smooth-edged leaflets and whitish/grayish hanging fruit clusters. Unlike staghorn sumac, it does not have upright red fuzzy cones and is tied to wet/boggy habitats.
records.edibility_detail: Not treated as an edible species in this guide. Included as a searchable caution/look-alike warning record.
records.look_alike_notes: Use this record to separate dangerous or not-recommended look-alikes from similar edible/tea-use records.

records.poisoning_effects: Not treated as food; dogbane contains toxic compounds and should be avoided as a milkweed substitute.
records.field_identification: Opposite leaves and milky sap can resemble milkweed, but dogbane often has more branching, slimmer stems, and narrower pods. Do not treat dogbane as edible milkweed.
records.edibility_detail: Not treated as an edible species in this guide. Included as a searchable caution/look-alike warning record.

records.habitat: Moist shady woods, stream edges, and rich forest sites.
records.edibility_detail: Not treated as an edible fiddlehead in this guide. Use as a warning comparison for ostrich fern.
records.notes: Fiddlehead look-alike warning record. Lady fern is common in damp woods, but it should not be treated as ostrich fern just because it has curled spring fronds.
records.general_notes: Fiddlehead look-alike warning record. Lady fern is common in damp woods, but it should not be treated as ostrich fern just because it has curled spring fronds.
records.culinary_uses: Not treated as an edible fiddlehead in this guide. Use as a warning comparison for ostrich fern.
records.medicinal_uses: No medicinal recommendation in this guide.
records.field_identification: Fern with delicate divided fronds; fiddleheads lack the clean ostrich-fern combination of deep U-shaped groove, smooth stem, and papery brown sheath pattern.
records.plant_card_note: Fern with delicate divided fronds; fiddleheads lack the clean ostrich-fern combination of deep U-shaped groove, smooth stem, and papery brown sheath pattern.
